section.title: My Heart Is Steadfast
section.sectionRationale: Written while David fled from Saul into a cave, likely at Adullam or En Gedi, 1 Samuel 22:1, 24:3. David takes refuge in the shadow of God's wings, and moves from surrounded danger to a fixed resolve to praise, framed by the refrain be exalted, O God, above the heavens, may Your glory cover all the earth, also found in Psalm 108.
section.thesis: Hiding from Saul in a cave, David takes refuge in the shadow of God's wings, and even surrounded by lions moves from fear to fixed praise, longing for God's glory to fill the whole earth, a longing answered when Christ's glory is proclaimed to every nation.
section.frameNote: Four movements: refuge in the shadow of God's wings amid danger; the surrounding lions and the enemies' own trap turned against them; the settled resolve, my heart is steadfast; and the closing refrain of praise among the nations, be exalted, O God, above the heavens.

units.label: In the shadow of Your wings

verses.text: Have mercy on me, O God, have mercy, for in You my soul takes {{chaseh|refuge}}. In the shadow of Your wings I will take shelter until the danger has passed.
I cry out to God Most High, to God who fulfills His purpose for me.
He reaches down from heaven and saves me; He rebukes those who trample me. Selah God sends forth His {{chesed|loving devotion}} and His truth.

units.label: My soul is among lions
units.note: surrounded, yet the enemies fall into their own trap

verses.text: My soul is among the lions; I lie down with ravenous beasts, with men whose teeth are spears and arrows, whose tongues are sharp swords.
Be exalted, O God, above the heavens; may Your {{kavod|glory}} cover all the earth.
They spread a net for my feet; my soul was despondent. They dug a pit before me, but they themselves have fallen into it! Selah

units.label: My heart is steadfast, O God

verses.text: My heart is steadfast, O God, my heart is steadfast. I will sing and make music.

Awake, my glory! Awake, O harp and lyre! 
I will awaken the dawn.

units.label: Be exalted, O God, above the heavens
units.note: the refrain of praise among the nations

verses.text: I will praise You, O Lord, among the nations; I will sing Your praises among the peoples.
For Your loving devotion reaches to the heavens, and Your faithfulness to the clouds.
Be exalted, O God, above the heavens; may Your glory cover all the earth.

chaseh.gloss: To take refuge, to seek shelter. David's soul takes refuge in God as a bird shelters under a parent's wings, an image of vulnerable, trusting dependence rather than self-reliant strength, echoed in Ruth 2:12 and taken up by Jesus Himself in Matthew 23:37.

chesed.gloss: Steadfast, covenant loving devotion, appearing in verses 3 and 10, the quality of God's character David trusts to reach down and save him and to extend as high as the heavens themselves.

kavod.gloss: Glory, weight, honor. The refrain's prayer, may Your glory cover all the earth, longs for God's own weighty, visible splendor to be acknowledged by every nation, not merely for David's personal rescue.

basic.context: Hiding from Saul in a cave, David pictures himself sheltering in the shadow of God's wings like a frightened bird, even while surrounded by enemies as dangerous as lions with teeth like spears. Remarkably, the psalm's refrain is not merely a plea for personal safety but a prayer that God's glory would cover the whole earth. By the psalm's second half, David's fear has become fixed, steadfast praise, and his focus has widened from his own danger to God's fame among all nations.
basic.hermeneutics: This psalm shows that genuine faith does not deny danger, David is quite literally among lions, but it does reframe the purpose of deliverance. David does not merely want to be saved; he wants God's glory to be seen by the whole earth. The move from anxious cry to settled resolve, my heart is steadfast, shows that fixed praise is often a deliberate choice made in the midst of danger, not only its eventual result.
basic.originalLanguages: David describes his soul as chasah, taking refuge, sheltering in the shadow of God's wings like a helpless bird. He trusts God's chesed, steadfast loving devotion, to reach down and save him, and prays that God's kavod, weighty glory, would cover the whole earth, not merely rescue him personally.
basic.typology: The wing-refuge image echoes the picture of God gathering His people as a bird gathers her young, taken up directly by Jesus in His lament over Jerusalem, and the prayer that God's glory cover the whole earth anticipates the Great Commission and the final filling of the earth with the knowledge of God's glory.
basic.christ: David's image of sheltering under God's wings is echoed by Jesus Himself, who laments over Jerusalem, how often I have longed to gather your children together, as a hen gathers her chicks under her wings, Matthew 23:37, revealing that the shelter David sought in God is offered personally by Christ. The refrain's longing, be exalted, O God, above the heavens, may Your glory cover all the earth, is set in motion by the Great Commission, Matthew 28:18-20, and will be finally and fully realized when the earth is filled with the knowledge of the glory of the LORD, Habakkuk 2:14, at Christ's return.
basic.god: God is shown as a refuge as intimate and tender as a parent bird's wing, and as sovereign and exalted as the heavens themselves, reaching down personally to save while remaining worthy of praise among every nation on earth.
basic.crossReferences: Ruth 2:12 blesses Ruth for taking refuge under the wings of the LORD. Matthew 23:37 shows Jesus longing to gather Jerusalem as a hen gathers her chicks under her wings. Habakkuk 2:14 promises the earth will be filled with the knowledge of the glory of the LORD, the ultimate answer to this psalm's refrain.
basic.oneStory: Surrounded by danger, David shelters in the shadow of God's wings and moves from fear to steadfast praise, longing not merely for his own rescue but for God's glory to cover the whole earth. That longing is embodied by Christ, who gathers His people under His own care, and will be finally fulfilled when His glory fills the earth at His return.

context.literary: A psalm of trust structured around a refrain, verses 5 and 11, be exalted, O God, above the heavens, may Your glory cover all the earth, framing an account of danger and refuge, verses 1-4, 6, and a central pivot to settled, musical praise, verses 7-8.
context.historical: The heading connects this psalm to David's flight from Saul into a cave, most likely Adullam or En Gedi, 1 Samuel 22:1, 24:3, moments when David had Saul within reach yet refused to harm the LORD's anointed, trusting God rather than taking vengeance himself.
context.canonical: Shares its closing refrain, verses 7-11, nearly word for word with Psalm 108:1-5, showing how psalms of personal trust were reused and combined for the wider worship life of God's people.

hermeneutics.authorIntent: To express refuge and trust in God amid real, surrounding danger, and to move deliberately from anxious plea to fixed, resolved praise that looks beyond personal rescue toward God's glory being known among all nations.
hermeneutics.descriptionVsPrescription: The psalm describes David's specific experience of danger and refuge in a cave; it prescribes an enduring pattern of faith, taking refuge in God and choosing steadfast praise even before deliverance is complete.

debates.question: Is David's declaration, 'my heart is steadfast,' a description of an emotional state he already possesses, or a resolve he is actively choosing?

views.label: A description of settled confidence already given by God amid the danger
views.case: The repetition, 'my heart is steadfast, my heart is steadfast,' reads naturally as a confident testimony to a settled state God has already produced in him through the refuge described in verses 1-3.

views.label: An act of the will, a deliberate resolve chosen in the midst of ongoing danger
views.case: The psalm has just described surrounding lions and a despondent soul, verses 4 and 6, suggesting the steadfastness of verse 7 is a decisive choice to fix the heart on praise despite, not because, circumstances have changed.

debates.bearing: Either reading shows that praise in this psalm is not dependent on the removal of danger; whether given or chosen, steadfastness of heart precedes the psalm's final resolution, modeling worship that does not wait for ideal circumstances.

study.originalLanguages: David describes his soul as chasah, taking refuge, sheltering under the shadow of God's wings like a vulnerable bird. He trusts God's chesed, steadfast loving devotion, to reach down and save him, and prays that God's kavod, weighty glory, would cover the whole earth, not merely secure his own rescue.

typology.type: Refuge in the shadow of God's wings, verse 1
typology.fulfillment: Jesus, who laments longing to gather Jerusalem's children under His wings as a hen gathers her chicks, Matthew 23:37, offering personally the shelter David sought in God.

typology.type: The prayer that God's glory would cover all the earth, verses 5 and 11
typology.fulfillment: The Great Commission, Matthew 28:18-20, and the final filling of the earth with the knowledge of God's glory at Christ's return, Habakkuk 2:14.

study.christ: David's image of sheltering under God's wings is echoed directly by Jesus, who laments over Jerusalem, how often I have longed to gather your children together, as a hen gathers her chicks under her wings, Matthew 23:37, showing that the shelter David sought in God is offered personally in Christ. The refrain's longing, be exalted, O God, above the heavens, may Your glory cover all the earth, is set in motion through the Great Commission, Matthew 28:18-20, and will be fully realized when the earth is filled with the knowledge of the glory of the LORD at Christ's return, Habakkuk 2:14.
study.god: God is a refuge as intimate as a parent bird's wing and as exalted as the heavens themselves, reaching down personally to save while remaining worthy of praise among every nation on earth.

crossReferences.note: Ruth is blessed for taking refuge under the wings of the LORD, the same refuge imagery David uses here.
Jesus longs to gather Jerusalem's children as a hen gathers her chicks under her wings.
The earth will be filled with the knowledge of the glory of the LORD, the ultimate answer to this psalm's refrain.

study.oneStory: Surrounded by danger, David shelters in the shadow of God's wings and moves from fear to steadfast praise, longing not merely for his own rescue but for God's glory to cover the whole earth. That longing is embodied by Christ, who gathers His people under His own care, and will be finally fulfilled when His glory fills the earth at His return.

quiz.q: What historical situation is this psalm connected to?

options.text: David hiding from Saul in a cave.
options.note: The heading ties this psalm to David's flight from Saul into a cave, 1 Samuel 22:1 or 24:3.

options.text: David's victory celebration after defeating the Ammonites.
options.note: This psalm describes danger and refuge, not a victory celebration.

options.text: David's coronation in Hebron.
options.note: The psalm's setting is a cave during flight from Saul, not a coronation ceremony.

options.text: Solomon's dedication of the temple.
options.note: This is a psalm of David during his flight, not a psalm connected to Solomon's temple dedication.

quiz.q: What image does David use in verse 1 to describe his trust in God?

options.text: Taking shelter in the shadow of God's wings, like a bird sheltering under a parent.
options.note: This vivid image of vulnerable, trusting refuge opens the psalm.

options.text: Standing behind a fortified stone wall.
options.note: Fortress imagery appears in other psalms, but this specific verse uses the image of wings, not walls.

options.text: Riding into battle on a war horse.
options.note: The image used is one of shelter and vulnerability, not of military action.

options.text: Climbing to the top of a high mountain alone.
options.note: The specific image in verse 1 is refuge under wings, not a mountain ascent.

quiz.q: What surprising turn happens in verse 6 regarding David's enemies?

options.text: They dug a pit and spread a net for David, but fell into the trap they set themselves.
options.note: This is stated explicitly, a moment of poetic justice within the psalm.

options.text: They repented and became David's allies.
options.note: The text describes their own trap turning against them, not a reconciliation.

options.text: They successfully captured David.
options.note: The opposite happens; their scheme backfires and they themselves fall into the trap.

options.text: They were defeated in an open military battle.
options.note: The verse describes a trap turned against its makers, not a battlefield confrontation.

quiz.q: What is the refrain that opens and closes the psalm's second half (verses 5 and 11)?

options.text: Be exalted, O God, above the heavens; may Your glory cover all the earth.
options.note: This refrain frames verses 5-11 and expresses a longing beyond personal rescue, for God's glory among all nations.

options.text: In God I trust, I shall not be afraid.
options.note: That refrain belongs to Psalm 56, not this psalm's refrain.

options.text: The Lord is my shepherd, I shall not want.
options.note: That refrain belongs to Psalm 23, not Psalm 57.

options.text: Cast your burden upon the Lord.
options.note: That is the key line of Psalm 55, not this psalm's refrain.

quiz.q: How does Jesus' teaching in Matthew 23:37 connect to this psalm?

options.text: Jesus echoes the wing-refuge image, longing to gather Jerusalem's children as a hen gathers her chicks under her wings.
options.note: This directly parallels David's image of sheltering under God's wings in verse 1.

options.text: Jesus rejects all imagery of refuge and shelter as unspiritual.
options.note: Jesus Himself uses very similar imagery of gathering and sheltering in Matthew 23:37.

options.text: Jesus applies this only to birds, not to people.
options.note: Jesus applies the image of a bird's shelter specifically to His desire to gather and protect people, Jerusalem's children.

options.text: Jesus uses it to condemn David's use of the image.
options.note: Jesus uses the same kind of image approvingly of His own longing to shelter people, not to condemn David's use of it.

questions: David pictures himself sheltering in the shadow of God's wings like a vulnerable bird, even while surrounded by real danger. What would it look like for you to trust God as an intimate refuge, rather than relying on your own strength, in a current difficulty?
David's enemies fell into the very trap they set for him. Where have you seen, in your own life or in Scripture, evil ultimately undoing itself under God's providence?
David moves from anxious plea to a deliberate declaration, my heart is steadfast. What would it mean for you to choose steadfast praise today, even before your circumstances change?

The psalm's refrain longs for God's glory to cover the whole earth, not merely for David's personal safety. How does shifting your prayers from purely personal concerns to God's global glory change the way you pray?

Jesus Himself uses the image of gathering His people under His wings, as David sought shelter under God's. How does knowing Christ personally offers this same refuge change how you come to Him in your fears?

translationNotes.note: Lions is used figuratively for fierce human enemies, a common ancient Near Eastern image for danger and predatory hostility, not a reference to literal wild animals in this context.
Awake, my glory is best understood as David rousing his own inner self, his glory or honor, to praise, rather than referring to an external object; some render it 'awake, my soul.'
This closing section is nearly identical to Psalm 108:1-5, suggesting these verses functioned as an independent unit of praise reused in the compilation of the Psalter.

meta.notes: BSB text fetched via the makor-scripture toolkit for Psalm 57:1-11. One em dash present in the raw BSB text at verse 4 was converted to a comma. No sermon from the trusted cluster (Begg, Keller, Piper, Ferguson, Carson) confirmed for Psalm 57 this session; sermon status set to none.
